title: Modal verbs: choose the right modal
lesson: Modal verbs (can, could, must, should, may, might…) add meaning to a main verb: ability, permission, obligation, or possibility. They are followed by the base verb with no 'to' (can swim), don't change with the subject, and form negatives directly (can't, mustn't).

items.explanation: 'mustn't' expresses prohibition — it is not allowed.

items.text: I {1} swim when I was five, but now I {2} ride a bicycle as well.
items.explanation: 'could' is the past form of 'can' for ability; 'can' expresses present ability.

items.text: You {1} see a doctor — that cough {2} be serious.
items.explanation: 'should' gives advice; 'might' expresses possibility — it may or may not be serious.

items.text: Students {1} bring their ID to the exam.
items.explanation: 'must' expresses strong obligation or a rule.

items.text: It's getting dark; you {1} drive carefully, you {2} use your headlights, and you {3} exceed the speed limit.
items.explanation: 'should' for advice (drive carefully); 'must' for obligation (use headlights — it's required); 'mustn't' for prohibition (exceeding the limit is forbidden).
